data.title: Green Debt Market Passes $3 Trillion Milestone
data.description:
By Alan Meng, Research Lead, Sustainable Fixed Income | Lee Clements, Director, Applied SI Research, FTSE Russell

Green bonds show resilience with Europe and Americas down offset by APAC surge

Green fixed income markets entered 2025 under a cloud of uncertainty, with headwinds from macroeconomics, political polarisation and geopolitical tensions[1]. But three quarters into the year, as the dust begins to settle, green bonds have continued to demonstrate resilience.

By the end of Q3, 2025 green bond issuance reached US$467 billion[2] – up 1% from the same period last year and keeping 2024’s full year record of US$572 billion within reach. This has been helped by a healthy overall market for debt issuance, with the share of green bonds relative to total global debt issuances dipping only slightly from 4.5% to 4.3%.[3]

As issuance momentum continued, the total outstanding for green bonds exceeded US$3 trillion at the end of Q3 2025 for the first time.[4] This means the global green bond outstanding universe has expanded at around 30% compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) over the past 5 years – underscoring the accelerating demand for climate finance to support mitigation and resilience, as highlighted in the recent LSEG Investing in the Green Economy 2025 report.

Figure 1 - Green bond issuances show resilience amid volatility

A slump in US corporates and soft issuance in Europe offset by strength in Asia

Europe remained the dominant source of global green bond issuance in 2025, with US$256 billion issued (or 55% of the total volumes), even if year-to-date (YTD) issuance is down 5% year-on-year. In the Americas, issuance dropped even more sharply - down 13% from the same period last year. In the U.S., corporate green bond issuance plunged by nearly 60%, but the municipal segment showed notable resilience, rising 30% and keeping overall U.S. issuance roughly on par with the levels reached during the same period last year. The slowdown across these regions was offset by resurgent issuance in the Asia-Pacific, led by China, where onshore green bond issuance doubled year-on-year.

Green bond markets continue to innovate and diversify

Corporate issuers – both listed and privately held – continue to lead the green bond market. They accounted for about two-thirds of 2025 issuance, with Financials, Utilities and Industrials in the lead. Sovereigns, too, relied on green bonds to mobilise resources in innovative ways. In April, China issued its first-ever sovereign green bond on the London Stock Exchange’s Sustainable Bond Market (SBM).[5] Denmark also launched the first sovereign bond under the new European Green Bond (EuGB) Standard,[6] structured as a ‘green twin bond’. This concept was pioneered by the German government and allows investors to observe price differences between green and conventional bonds.[7]

Use-of-proceeds data also shows that green debt markets continue to diversify. While mitigation themes such as renewable energy, energy efficiency and low-carbon transport still dominate, over a quarter of the eligible use-of-proceeds categories in the overall green bond market relate in some form to adaptation and resilience investments.[8] In the UK, for example, around 12% of its green gilt proceeds support flood and coastal-erosion management.[9]

Sustainable bond funds continue to show steady inflows, with green bond performance aligning closely with conventional bonds

The performance of green bonds – as measured by FTSE World Broad Investment-Grade Green Impact Bond Index (Green WBIG) – continues to closely track its benchmark, the FTSE World Broad Investment-Grade Bond Index (WBIG), despite slight underperformance year to date (see Figure 2). Note that the relative performance of green bond portfolios is generally sensitive to rate increases due to their positive active duration profiles underpinned by a higher concentration of long-duration government green bonds.

Figure 2 - Green bonds performance since 2018 (29 Dec 2017=100)

Sustainable bond fund flows have also continued to show resilience. According to LSEG Lipper data, 46 of the past 60 months have recorded net inflows – a strong signal of sustained demand, broadly mirroring the recovery of the wider fixed-income market following the 2022 sell-off. In March and April 2025, the segment saw brief outflows amid heightened volatility. However, sentiment quickly recovered, with May posting the second-highest monthly inflows of the past five years. Overall, since October 2020, sustainable bond funds have attracted cumulative net inflows of US$54 billion, underscoring enduring investor confidence in green and sustainable fixed-income strategies.

Figure 3 - Sustainable bond fund saw US$54 billion net inflows over 60 months

As 2025 draws to a close, the green bond market continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience - despite prevailing uncertainties and slowing growth in some regions. While short-term issuance and performance may remain influenced by macroeconomic conditions, the underlying fundamentals - rising financing needs for climate-mitigation and adaptation infrastructure[10], strong investor appetite, and stabilising performance in line with the broader fixed-income market - suggest that green bonds will remain a cornerstone of sustainable finance portfolios.

Footnotes

[1] FTSE Russell, LSEG, Sustainable Investment Insights Report 2024 Q4.

[2] Note the issuance figures are sourced from LSEG fixed income databases, which cover different asset classes, including corporate and government bonds, securitisations and US municipal bonds. Specifically, the annual issuance data is derived from Deal Intelligence and US Municipal Bond databases, and outstanding volumes are based on GovCorp database.

[3] Green bond ratio represents the principal amount of green bonds as a share of the overall newly issued bonds in each year, and it does not include US Muni bonds and securitisations.

[4] Cumulative green bond issuance reached approximately US$ 3.5 trillion as of Q3 2025, covering labelled green bonds, green securitisations, and green US municipal bonds, etc. Figures may differ across other market sources due to variations in data coverage, classification and eligibility criteria.

[5] LSEG, Ministry of Finance of the People’s Republic of China launches inaugural Sovereign Green Bonds Issuance.

[6] Danmarks Nationalbank, The Government has issued a 10-year green bond under the EuGB framework.

[7] Finance Agency, the Federal Republic of Germany, Twin Bond Concept - Deutsche Finanzagentur.

[8] LSEG, Green Economy Report - Investing in the green economy 2025

[9] United Kingdom Debt Management Office, UK Green Financing Allocation Report - October 2024.

[10] According to Climate Bonds Initiative, a total of US$17.8 trillion sustainable bonds is to be issued globally by 2035. The $4.4trn Climate Investment Opportunity.

data.title: Why Canopy Growth Is No Longer A Strong Sell
data.description:
I have been very negative on Canopy Growth Corporation (CGC) for a long time. The stock is still above the all-time low that was set in April, and it is also above the recent low that was set in late July, but it has dropped a lot since its peak in August. I have had a Strong Sell since late May, ahead of their Q4 report, which I reiterated in June after the report, and I have been negative on CGC here at Seeking Alpha since early 2023, when I said it was not a good stock for cannabis investors to own. While it still is likely not, I am upgrading it to Hold.

In my last piece in June, when the stock was at $1.73, I reiterated my Strong Sell, concluding that other cannabis stocks made more sense. In this piece, I explain why the potential downside risk (bankruptcy) that I have envisioned is unlikely to happen.

The Business Is Still Not Good

The company has now reported its Q1 and Q2 for FY26, which ends in March. Analysts, according to Koyfin, were projecting revenue of C$71.8 million and adjusted EBITDA of -C$3.8 million when they reported Q2 on 11/09. The company's net revenue of C$66.9 million was below expectations, but it rose from a year earlier by 6%. I like that the company refocused and is just a cannabis company now. It reports two segments, Cannabis and Storz & Bickel (ancillary devices). Cannabis revenue grew 12% to C$50.9 million. Adult-use and medical cannabis sales in Canada increased, but international sales fell 39% to just C$5.1 million. Storz & Bickel was the remaining C$15.8 million, falling 10%.

Adjusted EBITDA was a bit better than expected at -C$3.0 million, and it improved from a year ago, when it was -C$5.5 million. It was also much better than the Q1 adjusted EBITDA of -C$7.9 million.

Cash flow from operations has been a big challenge for Canopy Growth, and it had an off quarter, with the use in its operations of C$18 million. This was worse sequentially, as it was -C$10.3 million in Q1, but it was a lot better than a year ago, when cash flow from operations was -C$53.8 million. In the first half of the fiscal year, the -C28.3 million compared to a cash burn of C$105.6 million a year ago.

Many investors have perhaps been excited about the moves by Canopy Growth into the American cannabis market with the acquisitions of Acreage Holdings, Jetty Extracts, and Wana Brands made by Canopy USA, but those businesses remain off the income statement and are reflected only on the balance sheet as an investment. Canopy Growth has made no progress with the NASDAQ, which forced the company to set up a structure that allowed it to hold these acquisitions but not give up its NASDAQ listing. While I am not optimistic that things will change, perhaps they will. More importantly, perhaps the U.S. will end up eliminating 280E taxation. With that said, the recent legislation to ban THC from hemp is a challenge to the Canopy Growth businesses. Perhaps this law will not be implemented as scheduled in a year.

The Balance Sheet Got Better

The big story of Q2 was that the company moved from having net debt to having net cash. It ended Q2 with a reported C$298 million in cash and total debt outstanding of C$228 million, but the cash was actually higher when the company reported due to stock sales that were completed after the quarter ended.

I was shocked to see how quickly the company moved to fix its balance sheet, though I had expected it to issue stock. The company's 10-Q reveals that CGC sold a lot of stock during its fiscal Q2 and also sold 9.8 million shares in fiscal Q3 for gross proceeds of C$19.8 million. The bad news is that the share count has soared to 342.2 million as of 11/6. I calculate about 372 million shares on a fully diluted basis, including 26.3 million Exchangeable Shares and 3.7 million RSUs that are outstanding. The good news is that the company has about C$89.5 million in net cash now.

The Valuation Is No Longer So Challenging

When I wrote after the Q4 financials were released, the stock was trading at 1.4X tangible book value, which was a premium to its peers then. It trades now at 0.9X. More importantly, perhaps, the company is no longer drowning in debt. The cash flow is still negative, but the sale of stock by the company has improved the balance sheet, as I discussed above.

Ahead of the report, analysts, according to Koyfin, were projecting FY27 revenue of C$318.2 million and adjusted EBITDA of C$19.7 million. Now, they forecast revenue to grow 7% to C$302.3 million, a bit lower but still higher than FY25 and FY26. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be C$13.9 million, which is up from -C$13.2 million projected for FY26. The stock is no bargain, but it looks to be finally moving to positive adjusted EBITDA. Relative to the FY27 outlook, the enterprise value of US$309 million is 31X projected adjusted EBITDA for FY27.

The Chart Could Go Either Way

The reverse splits can sometimes confuse investors, but Canopy Growth is way down. It is currently down 61% in 2025, which is a lot worse than the cannabis sector, which has declined by 14.1% for the Global Cannabis Stock Index that we maintain at New Cannabis Ventures. In the past 5 years, CGC has dropped over 99%:

Of course, most cannabis stocks have plunged over the past five years. Looking at the action over the past year, the stock has been volatile:

At $1.07, the stock is 39% higher than its all-time low set in April. The surge in volume since August is due to two things. First, people got very excited about the stock when American cannabis rescheduling was being discussed again in the media. Second, though, the huge sale of stock by the company in Q3 and the beginning of Q4 through its ATM Shelf has boosted trading volumes.

To me, the stock looks to be bottoming technically. The stock is testing $1.00, which can be a critical spot for companies listed on the NASDAQ, as failure to trade above $1.00 can lead to delisting risk. Canopy has reverse-split a couple of times already and will likely do so again if necessary. I don't view this as inherently bad, but some do.

There are 7 stocks that are Canadian LPs currently in the Global Cannabis Stock Index, and CGC has been the worst performer over the past three years by far:

Poor stock performance is not a reason to buy for a reversal in my view. I continue to view Tilray Brands, Inc. (TLRY), which is also down a lot (though up a lot recently) negatively, and, while I have been wrong recently to be negative on Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF), I remain so.

Conclusion

Canopy Growth is off the deathwatch. I am not suggesting that readers actually buy Canopy Growth, but I think that those who want to own it could make money doing so or could lose it. The stock has come down a lot, but the risk of bankruptcy has been reduced. There are still better stocks to own, but there are riskier stocks, too.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NL Industries: Majority-Owned CompX Offsetting Kronos Concerns
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on NL Industries, Inc. (NL) in July 2025, when we rated the industrial holding company a 'Hold.' Shares were trading at approximately $6.70 at the time, which means they are down roughly 18% from a capital depreciation standpoint. It must be pointed out, however, that our commentary on NL revolves around the stock being an excellent long-term income play. As mentioned previously, lower prices in above-average dividend-paying stocks enable compounding to occur at a faster clip over time. Therefore, despite the downturn in NL in recent months, our job is to ascertain whether the company's fundamentals remain intact so the company can continue to pay out excellent dividend distributions to its shareholders.

To recap, NL Industries operates as a holding company that owns a majority stake in CompX International Inc. (CIX) plus a non-controlling stake in Kronos Worldwide, Inc. (KRO). As stated in our July commentary, the rationale behind our 'Hold' rating was that, as long as NL stock continues to trade within the trading range outlined below, we believed the stock offered good value as a long-term dividend play, with the emphasis being on 'long-term.' Remember, companies such as NL that consistently pay out large amounts of capital from either their generated cash flows or balance-sheet cash will rarely see significant capital appreciation in their respective stock over time. It is all about the investor's 'total return potential over the long term,' so this will be the premise of this article from this point forward.

NL's Real Dividend Yield & Real Returns Are Much Higher

NL's stated dividend yield is $0.36 per year, which equates to a yield of 6.57%. Many times, income-oriented investors use the dividend yield as a barometer of whether the stock is cheap or not at a given point in time. Therefore, NL's payout looks favourable here, given its own 5-year average yield amounts to a lower 4.11%, with the sector in general currently yielding only 1.49%.

Moreover, NL paid out a special dividend this year of $0.21 per share in August this year, meaning the real trailing dividend yield in this play comes in at an impressive 10.4%. Therefore, when we consider that the stock has declined approximately 18% in price over the past 4 or so months, the real negative return for shareholders over this period is closer to approximately 15%.

However, it is the ongoing payment of special dividends that significantly boosts returns over longer periods, so this is where investors should keep their focus. We initially rated NL a 'Buy' in September 2020 when shares were trading at $4.26 a share. The capital gain increase since that article amounts to approximately 26%, whereas the total return for shareholders during this period comes in at a much higher 79%. This is due to the payment of two generous special dividends of $0.35 per share in 2022 and $0.43 per share in 2024. Suffice it to say, if you hang around long enough in NL, returns should amplify in your favour.

Q3 Earnings

NL released its Q3 earnings on 11/6/2025, where a GAAP loss of -$7.8 million on revenues of approximately $40 million for the 3 months. Although COMPX reported higher segment profit for the period ($4.8 million as opposed to $3.3 million in Q3 of 2024), equity in losses of Kronos to the tune of $11.3 million meaningfully adversely affected Q3 numbers, especially compared to the same quarter of 12 months prior.

From a dividend perspective, however, it is important to note that cash pays distributions and not income tallies on an income statement (which contains non-cash charges). To the above point, despite the disappointing loss in Q3, NL still managed to deliver $7.9 million of positive operating cash flow due to tailwinds in the likes of depreciation and inventory. Although NL's operating cash flow did not cover all dividend payments in the quarter ($14.7 million), this was due to the elevated special dividend, which, as mentioned above, totalled an additional $0.21 per share. This means almost $9 million was used from NL's cash balance, resulting in a closing cash balance of $101.9 million at the end of the company's recent third quarter.

Strong Performance of CompX

As mentioned, NL holds a majority stake in the Security & Protection Services outfit CompX International Inc. From a long-term perspective, once more, we recommend investors consider not only digesting CompX's recent set of quarterly earnings results but also how the company's first three quarters this year compared to the same period in fiscal 2024.

To the above point, CompX's net sales increased by $6.4 million and $13.1 million, respectively, in Q3 and for the first three quarters of fiscal 2024. From a fundamental standpoint, it was encouraging to see both segments doing very well in Q3 and the first nine months (higher sales in Security Products & Marine Components), resulting in higher segment profit in both periods, respectively ($4.8 million & $11.9 million). Strength in the government security market, as well as the gas station security market, continues to drive sales in the larger Security products segment, irrespective of transportation and tool storage softness at this time.

The smaller 'Marine Components' segment grew its sales by 36% and 29% in Q3 and for the first nine months, respectively, with the towboat, government, and industrial markets remaining to the fore. Management is confident of the profitability outlook in this segment, as we see from management's Q3 earnings report comments below.

CompX expects Marine Components' net sales to increase in 2025, predominantly due to higher expected sales to the government and industrial markets, as well as the one-time stocking event for a towboat OEM customer in the first quarter of 2025. Overall, CompX expects the Marine Components reporting unit to have improved gross margin and reporting unit percentages in 2025 compared to 2024 due to increased coverage of fixed costs on higher expected sales volumes.

Kronos Concerns

Kronos Worldwide, in its recent third quarter, reported a negative GAAP print of -$37 million, resulting in a net loss of $28.1 million over the first three quarters of the present fiscal year. Both tallies are well down compared to the 2024 comparable periods. Kronos is an international producer of TiO2, where, unfortunately, market conditions for some time now have not been cooperative. Therefore, Kronos' production capacity utilisation rates continue to contract as the company continues to endeavour to downsize its costs by better aligning production schedules to demand.

Nevertheless, there are a few areas where we see Kronos being able to make gains in a difficult market. Firstly, customer inventory levels have been low for some time and cannot remain so indefinitely. Secondly, given the depressed nature of the TiO2 market, many shutdowns and facility closures have taken place in Europe and China, for example. Now, customers who availed of this production before may still require TiO2 but now have to source it elsewhere. Remember, even in down markets, the best players many times have the wherewithal to gain market share if they can run a tight ship. Thirdly, in time, we believe the acquisition with respect to taking full control of LPC in July last year positions Kronos to take advantage when the market inevitably turns in the North American market.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in NL Industries due to continued rangebound technicals, a keen valuation where stock is trading for less than three times its cash position, and CompX strength, which continues to compensate for Kronos weakness at this point. Keep reinvesting these quarterly payouts and specials. Where the intention must be to stay invested for the long term. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Banco do Brasil Q3 Earnings: Nothing Constructive To Hold On To
data.description:
Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY) is going through a dark period, with profits and credit quality plummeting, tied to a credit crisis in the Brazilian agribusiness, the most representative sector for the bank.

While still carrying a ~10% gain over the course of this year, since mid-May, shares were down as much as 37% by August, although they have partially recovered, having fallen 23% since the peak this year.

In my last coverage of the thesis, I adopted a bearish stance on the ADR:

"I'm downgrading Banco do Brasil to underperform after the Brazilian state-owned bank posted a very weak quarter in 2Q25, following an atrocious 1Q25 and soft guidance in 4Q24."

Since then, little or nothing has positively evolved in the thesis, in my opinion. In fact, Banco do Brasil has entered a cycle of progressive deterioration, culminating in a 3Q25 that reinforces that there are no concrete signs of stabilization in rural credit or in the cost of credit.

Although the capital quality of the Brazilian state-owned bank remains robust and far from any solvency stress, operational performance continues to deteriorate, and risk remains concentrated precisely in the segment where the bank is most exposed: Brazilian agribusiness.

With the dividend thesis behind us and the ADR having recovered part of its post-2Q25 fall, the risk-return asymmetry has become less favorable. If the valuation previously offered a more solid margin of safety by trading at around 60% of its book value, today trading at 74% of book value seems a fairer price for a bank that (1) keeps revising its guidance downwards; (2) reports successive increases in defaults; and (3) has its cost of credit pushed to the highest levels in decades.

Clinging to the expectation that defaults in agribusiness will only cool down in 1Q26 seems too little for now, in my view, for a re-rating of the thesis. And until there is clear evidence of at least stabilization of the main KPIs, I will remain skeptical/pessimistic.

A Crisis Timeline

Sketching out a timeline to establish a context of what has haunted Banco do Brasil this year, let's start with 1Q25.

In the quarter reported in May of this year, Banco do Brasil saw its adjusted net income reach R$7.4 billion, a drop of 20% YoY, with agribusiness delinquencies (NPL 90+) rising to close to 3% when just last year the levels were still at 1-1.5%. Agribusiness provisions have risen from R$3 billion in 4Q24 to ~R$5 billion, causing the cost of rural credit to jump sharply.

Under the hood, there was (1) a new rule on provisions for expected losses (Res. 4.966), which basically brought forward the recognition of the loss, making the PDD appear earlier in the result; (2) the beginning of the crisis in Brazilian agribusiness, in a phase of margin squeeze. Soybean and corn producers were suffering from weather problems, costs were still high due to post-shock inputs from 2022-2023, as well as a very high real interest rate on debts contracted in a cycle of optimism—interest rates (Selic) converging to the current 15% per year. It was then that we saw the start of a more intense wave of requests for judicial recovery in agribusiness.

Banco do Brasil revised its guidance downwards slightly, and the market priced in a bad cycle, albeit manageable.

Thus, shares collapsed from $5.44 to $3.43.

Then came 2Q25, with results reported in mid-August. Naturally, a weak quarter was anticipated, but no one expected the results to drop like an atom bomb. Net profit plummeted 60% YoY to R$3.8 billion, ROE fell from 21.6% in 3Q24 to 8.4%.

Agribusiness officially becomes the villain of the quarter, with Banco do Brasil reporting that it is facing the highest level of defaults in the history of the agribusiness portfolio, with NPL 90+ rising to 3.49% vs. 1.3% a year ago. And the detail is that 74% of the twenty thousand defaulting clients had no history of arrears until 2023, which shows that even reliable payers—rural producers—have been crushed by market conditions.

In the macro context, international grain prices have fallen well back from their peaks in 2021 and 2022, while the cost of production has taken longer to fall, and the Selic rate of 15% has continued to make the bill heavier. There was a boom in requests for judicial reorganization, reaching 808. As a result, the cost of credit soared in triple digits (104% YoY) and further 57% QoQ to R$15.9 billion.

The consequence was yet another downward revision of guidance, but this time for real, cutting the profit forecast and raising the cost of credit projection.

Despite reporting its worst quarterly result in many years, Banco do Brasil's ADR partially recovered post-Q2, considering a market interpretation that this could be a crisis concentrated in specific yields, that many rural producers are asking for judicial reorganization for liquidity, not structural insolvency, and above all, the fact that the ADR traded as low as 0.6x P/B and a P/E ratio of 5.6x—extremely de-risked for a bank that is not bankrupt, far from it.

3Q25 Breakdown

Speaking of the chances of bankruptcy, to make it clear that there is not the slightest risk of this happening, in 3Q25 Banco do Brasil reported BIS III of 14.8%, CET1 of 11.1%, and Tier 1 of 13.9%, figures well above the regulatory minimum and also well above a stress level. In fact, liquidity continues to be quite comfortable, including for the distribution of dividends—albeit now much lower.

But apart from that, 3Q25 was another horrible quarter.

To begin with, adjusted profit came in at R$3.8 billion, a further drop of 60% YoY and an ROE of the same paltry 8.4% as 2Q25.

Consolidated NPL 90+ came in at 4.93% (+160 bps YoY; +72 bps QoQ), and agribusiness jumped from 3.5% to 5.34% QoQ. The balance of total provisions against default reached R$35.3 billion, up 15.5% vs. 3Q24. Also, the balance of agribusiness clients in judicial reorganization rose from R$5.4 billion (June) to R$6.6 billion (September), with 120 new producers entering RJ just in the meantime.

The evolution of ALLL expenses in 3Q25, from 77% YoY and 12.7% QoQ to R$17.9 billion, also shows the amount of money that Banco do Brasil has had to keep to cover defaults, with R$8.8 billion coming from agro businesses, whereas in the first 9M24 it was only R$7.6 billion, showing the magnitude of this number.

That said, the cost of credit standing at 5.16% in 3Q25 highlights the operational suffering, but with no risk of capital collapse.

And of course, Banco do Brasil's management team slashed its guidance again. The adjusted net income expected for 2025 is now R$18-21 billion, compared to R$21-25 billion, and the expected cost of credit is now R$59-62 billion, compared to R$53-56 billion. The management team's commentary also pointed to an expected inflection in agribusiness delinquency only in 1Q26, making it clear that they are in the "eye of the hurricane".

Thus, Banco do Brasil finds itself in a situation, compared to its incumbent domestic peers, in which it is (1) the only one with a drop in annual profit; (2) the only one with a drop of more than 60% YoY; (3) the only one with an explosion in agribusiness delinquencies.

Updating the comparative table between the four main domestic incumbents, it's no coincidence that Banco do Brasil is lagging in practically all KPIs and has the poorest valuation of all, showing that the problem is not necessarily macro, but company-related, mainly due to its exposure to agribusiness, which its peers don't share to the same extent.

Final Remarks and Risks

I find it very difficult to hold any position other than bearish at the moment, as in the sphere of Banco do Brasil's results, nothing has improved—in fact, everything has worsened, or deteriorated further.

If the market was hoping for at least a semblance of stabilization in agribusiness NPLs, it didn't come, nor has the deceleration in the cost of credit. Any sign of inflection didn't show up, although the management team's indication of a likely turnaround in agribusiness delinquencies by 1Q26 offers a slightly positive point for expectations.

But other than that, (1) NPL 90+ jumped to 4.9%; (2) agribusiness NPL 90+ jumped to 5.3% vs. 3.5% in 2Q25; and (3) ALLL expenses rising again (R$17.9 billion), plus several other KPIs signaling deterioration, push the thesis anywhere but an upgrade.

Although for those who think more in the long term, it's worth noting here that the asymmetry has changed, and the valuation isn't as cheap as it was post-2Q25, as the ADR rose, partially correcting some of the panic. So here we have a situation where the risk continues to increase, but the discount has narrowed.

Even for those who consider that trading at ~70% of its book value is an opportunity, this discount also embeds the political risk that Banco do Brasil's state-owned thesis carries. This risk implies the need for Brazil's federal government to use the state-owned bank as a tool to rescue distressed state-owned companies, especially in a period where presidential elections are approaching in Brazil, specifically in 2026.

That said, I believe that 3Q25 was just one of a couple of quarters ahead of darkness for Banco do Brasil, and I would expect some positive signaling on 4Q25 to be constructive again in the thesis. Otherwise, I believe that BDORY's ADR could look cheap on the surface but prove very costly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arbor Realty Trust Preferreds: The Bizarre Price Gap
data.description:
Arbor Realty Trust's preferred stocks (ABR.PR.D)/(ABR.PR.E)/(ABR.PR.F) show durability as income investments, even as the issuer has had some recent distress. The Series D & E in particular offer the more attractive valuations of the preferred capitalizations and warrant a Buy rating.

Concerns Regarding The Common

Most potential interest in the Arbor's preferreds stems from their perception in relation to the common (ABR). Being a REIT, ABR is effectively an income security like a preferred stock, albeit with the riskier capitalization attributed to the common.

I wrote about ABR earlier in the month, expressing my concerns about the multifamily loans on their balance sheet.

The bulk of Arbor's assets are in these MF loans, and the table above shows the largest concentration in the 2021 and 2022 vintages. These were underwritten right at the start of the Fed's rapid interest rate hikes, which has caused most of them to fall below the Pass/Watch rating, into Special Mention, Substandard, and Doubtful.

CFO Paul Elenio stated in the Q3 earnings call:

As discussed earlier, the resolution of these loans will likely occur over several months, which will cause our quarterly earnings to fluctuate during that time period. We will do our best to give as much forward guidance as we can as it relates to any realized loss we will incur as we continue to make progress in resolving the bulk of our legacy book.

Realizing that such a large pile of assets needed a lot of work, likely with more write-offs, markdowns, and decreases in distributable income ahead, I cautiously rated the shares a Hold, in spite of the nominal discount to book value.

Preferred Series

Arbor's preferred series consists of three issues: D, E, and F.

They all carry similar coupons, at 6.375%, 6.25%, and 6.25%, respectively, making them comparable income opportunities on yield alone.

With a combined capitalization of $633.7M, they account for 20% of Arbor's total equity.

With about $41M in preferred dividend expense, they've typically been well-covered by Arbor's GAAP earnings, which are in excess of $200M for the last three years.

For the reported nine months of 2025 as well, earnings have continued to cover preferred dividends, in spite of the distress that forced the common dividend to be cut from $0.43 per quarter to $0.30 in Q2.

All three series are similar in that they carry par/liquidation values of $25, bear cumulative dividends, and are redeemable at the option of the company after a certain date in 2026. Series F is unique in that it has a fixed-to-floating dividend, based on SOFR plus a margin of 5.442%, with a floor of 6.125%. Importantly, they are "pari passu," equal in ranking. Series D is not senior to E or F in the capitalization, despite the implication.

Outlook And Valuation

These issues are more alike than they are different in their properties. Nevertheless, the market does not seem to price them in that way.

Series F currently trades much closer to par than D or E. Around when each series was first issued, they all traded much closer in price to each other. The main divergence occurred, strangely, in late 2024, well after the Fed had been raising rates (which would benefit F's floating dividend), and F appreciated well above D and E.

That gap has remained intact through the events of 2025. Series D & E trade under $18, for yields of about 9%, with Series F above $22 and yielding 7%.

I'll go ahead and state something that feels basic but needs to be said: Series F is not a Buy at these prices unless we believe rates will be increased.

Given the recent cuts by the Fed in September and October, I think that is unlikely at this moment. In fact, additional cuts seem more likely, with the real uncertainty being around pace and timing. With Chairman Powell's term ending next May as well, one has to consider what the priorities of a Trump appointee will be. The President has been critical of what he perceives as slowness on the part of the Fed to make cuts sooner, dubbing its chairman "Too Late Powell," and I think this suggests his nominee next year will be inclined toward additional cuts.

With 9% yields, Series D & E are viable buys with no additional risk over Series F. One has to believe that Arbor won't go bankrupt in its attempt to restructure and rotate its distressed MF loans. This is a possibility, and much uncertainty remains with over $4B in those underperforming 2021 & 2022 MF loans. CEO Ivan Kaufman described the situation for the next few quarters as such:

As this trend continues, we believe we will be able to meaningfully grow our origination volumes and start to move more assets off our balance sheet, which will increase our earnings run rate and position us well for the future. As I mentioned earlier, we've taken an aggressive approach to resolving our legacy assets through a myriad of different strategies, including modifying loans, taking back assets as REOs to own and operate and bringing in new sponsors to take over assets and assume our debt and create a more current income stream.

The most likely setback for the preferreds seems to be disruptions in net interest income that hurt GAAP earnings and force the preferred to accumulate dividends for a while.

As the common's distributions are almost six times the amount of preferred, however, there is much more room for this to be cut before the preferreds find themselves hindered. Since management hasn't completely eliminated the common dividend yet, I don't think we are quite there.

Arbor's liabilities draw on different forms of financing. These include a credit line, securitized debt through CLO vehicles, unsecured note, and a few smaller sources. This has given them some flexibility in managing the distress up to this point and can give them a little more wiggle room.

There are still $11.4B in loans held (not for sale) on the book, not all of which are distressed. These include $2.8B in single-family rentals, which have been significantly better-performing throughout the entire period of distress. It also reflects the newer-vintage MF loans that weren't underwritten with the same generous assumptions about the future as the lower-rate vintages of 2021 & 2022.

A failed preferred investment requires Arbor to fail to the point that the preferred equity is wiped out, and I think too many of the assets are healthy for that to occur. There is a modest case that dividends will be suspended and forced to accumulate, which is good protection for long-term investors, but I don't see Arbor going bankrupt unless its healthy loans somehow become unhealthy.

For that reason, I think income investors, even those looking for capital gains, can find reasonable buys in Series D & E.

Conclusion

Most preferred stocks are not worth a second look, either because the issuer is so healthy that they couldn't be better than the common or because the issuer is in such crisis that preferred status offers no meaningful protection. Arbor's preferreds seem to be in the Goldilocks Zone, where the common faces threats of additional dividend cuts, while the preferred either doesn't or has a long-term benefit of rescue through its cumulative feature.

As the D & E offer better yields and better discounts to par over F, with no additional risk in their structure, I rate the former two a Buy and the latter a Hold.

data.title: Gold's Meteoric Rise In 2025: A Safe Haven Amid Global Uncertainty
data.description:
By Erwan Jacob, Macro Analyst, LSEG

Gold has taken centre stage in 2025, soaring 50% and outperforming major asset classes. This insight explores why gold has become the ultimate safe haven amid global economic uncertainty, central bank buying, and geopolitical tensions.

This year, gold has been experiencing its strongest rally since 1979, when the Iranian Revolution disrupted the global economy. Since the start of 2025, the spot price of gold has risen by 50%, outpacing leading asset classes. Data from Bank of America shows that this momentum may continue, driven by structural demand from central banks and monetary easing initiated by the US Federal Reserve (Fed).

The loosening of the Fed's monetary policy comes at a pivotal moment as employment figures weaken while inflation remains sticky – 32,000 jobs in the US were lost in September. The pivot announced by Fed Chair Powell at Jackson Hole reflects a broader economic slowdown as the impact of trade disputes continues. Traditionally, the US Treasuries and the dollar have served as safe havens, but investor confidence has eroded amid ongoing challenges at the Fed and a global rebalancing of US trade policy. Gold has emerged as a natural safe haven asset amid the US dollar’s underperformance so far this year.

Furthermore, ongoing geopolitical developments in the Middle East and Eastern Europe are also fuelling the gold rally, especially as the previously optimistic stance of the Trump administration on resolving the Russia-Ukraine conflict has been abandoned amid stalled diplomatic talks. During the summer, the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act,' passed by Congress and signed into law by the President, has also impacted U.S. Treasuries. While the bill aims to stimulate the economy through tax cuts, the anticipated decline in government revenue is expected to strain the U.S. fiscal outlook.

Central banks, particularly in emerging markets, have increased the pace of gold purchases since 2022, when Russia’s foreign-currency reserves were frozen following its invasion of Ukraine. Emerging market central banks remain significantly underweight in gold compared to their developed market counterparts and are gradually increasing allocations as part of a broader diversification strategy.

Estimates indicate that China holds less than 10% of its reserves in gold, compared to around 70% for the US, Germany, France, and Italy. Central banks purchased less gold in July than in an average month this year, which aligns with seasonal patterns. Central bank purchases tend to slow in the summer and pick up again from September onwards. Notably, some 95% of surveyed central banks expect global gold holdings to increase in the next 12 months, with none anticipating a decline.

Another driver of gold demand is the weaker outlook for other hard currencies. After the collapse of the newly appointed French government and the French credit downgrade, the USD/EUR spot rate ticked higher. The French vs. German 10-year spread widened to its highest level since January, with the French 10Y yield trading 86 basis points above Germany’s. This is in the context of the yield on Germany’s 10Y Bund rising to 2.73%, tracking increases in European, US, and Japanese government bond yields as political turbulence reverberates through global markets.

The Euro area has not displayed signs of economic strength this year. Based on ECB forecasts, GDP growth is expected to reach 1.2% and headline inflation to settle at 2.1% annually. Estimates of the Euro trade balance showed a €12.4 bn surplus in trade in goods with the rest of the world in July 2025, compared with €18.5 bn in July 2024. Compared to July 2024, the Euro balance decreased by €6.1 bn, mainly attributed to chemicals and related products, which experienced a decline in surplus from €23.8 bn to €17.4 bn. Multiple policy rate cuts this year by the ECB sent the rate to 2% to accommodate the modest inflation growth outlook.

In Japan, the Yen fell to a six-month low after Sanae Takaichi’s leadership victory due to concerns regarding fiscal spending. Takaichi has pledged to boost the Japanese economy with aggressive spending and has been critical of the tightening of monetary policy by the Bank of Japan (BoJ). The economic slowdown triggered by trade disputes and the heavy reliance of the Japanese economy on exports has put the BoJ in a difficult situation, as a rate hike needed to curb inflation could potentially deter economic growth prospects. Japan’s finance minister said authorities were watching out for excessive moves in currency markets. Hence, the Yen could remain weak in the coming weeks, potentially opening the door for the resumption of the Yen carry trade – depending on the volatility in the market and the outcome of the next BoJ policy meeting.

GBP is also facing headwinds as the UK economy is met with multiple challenges. There are rumours that the current government could potentially increase taxes in order to cope with the rampant deficit. This comes in a challenging context, as the Bank of England decided to keep its policy rate unchanged during the last policy meeting held in September. Inflation remains sticky in the UK, close to 4%, while GDP growth in July was zero, down from 0.4% in June. Despite the volatility triggered by the trade decisions made by the US administration, US GDP should outpace UK GDP in 2025. The US is set to grow at about 1.8%, while the UK should grow at 1.2%, roughly at the same pace as the EU.

In summary, the status of the US dollar as the global reserve currency deteriorated due to several factors, such as US fiscal policy, US trade policy and geopolitical developments. Gold has become a beneficiary of the global economic turmoil, whereas the Euro, the British pound and the Yen are currently not considered to be reliable alternatives.

data.title: Uber: Nvidia Partnership Allays Any Fears Of The AV Threat (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Uber (UBER), I analysed the company’s Q2 report and examined the key takeaways from the report. I had a HOLD rating on the name.

Since the article was published, in August 2025, the company has gained just 0.43%, underperforming the S&P 500, which gained 4.42% during the same period.

UBER reported its Q3 earnings two weeks ago, and despite a strong quarter, the company’s shares tanked post the earnings release. In this article, I analyse why there was a divergence between the actual results and the stock-price action. I also evaluate what investors should look out for when it comes to UBER’s AV ambitions in 2026 and beyond.

A Snapshot Of The Q3 Earnings Report

UBER delivered yet another strong quarter, beating both the top- and bottom-line estimates once again. More specifically, Q3 revenues came in at $13.5 billion, up 20.5% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $240 million. Diluted EPS came in at $3.11, up a whopping 159% y/y and blowing past analyst estimates of $0.69. Investors must factor in that the significant EPS beat was primarily due to an additional $4.9 billion benefit that UBER gained from a “tax valuation release,” and a $1.5 billion pretax gain on account of revaluation of UBER’s equity investments.

Q3 gross bookings grew 21% y/y, coming in at $49.74 billion, matching the upper end of the company’s guidance. Adjusted EBITDA of $2.26 billion was also near the upper range of the company’s guidance, but its growth of 33% y/y was the lowest growth in the last five quarters. The company generated operating cash flows of $2.3 billion, ending the quarter with $9.1 billion in unrestricted cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments.

For the fourth quarter, the company expects gross bookings to come in between $52.25 and $53.75 billion, which assumes an approximately 1% currency tailwind. Adjusted EBITDA is now expected to be in the range between $2.41 and $2.51 billion, which would represent a y/y growth in the range between 31% and 36%.

UBER’s AV Partnerships Are Already Paying Off

One of the key takeaways for me, from UBER’s Q3 report, was how autonomous vehicles (AVs) are slowly but steadily contributing to the company’s bookings and, subsequently, to the company’s top line. This was seen in the growth in the number of trips, which jumped to 22% after being at 18% for three consecutive quarters. This is the strongest growth since the end of 2023, indicating that all the partnerships that UBER has made with various AV companies are starting to pay off. The partnership with Waymo is the biggest AV-contributor for UBER, as evidenced by Austin and Atlanta (where UBER has partnered with Waymo) growing at a faster rate than other U.S. markets.

While the AV market is still in its infancy, UBER is establishing a strong foundation that will allow it to capitalize when the market eventually delivers exponential growth. The clearest evidence of this is UBER’s partnership with (NVDA). Under the terms of the partnership, UBER plans to oversee end-to-end AV fleet operations, and the AVs will be developed on NVDA’s DRIVE AGX Hyperion platform. (STLA) will be the first automaker that will deliver at least 5,000 of these L4 autonomy vehicles for UBER’s robotaxi operations, both in the US and internationally.

Personally, I don’t see UBER managing its own AV fleet for the long-term. I continue to believe that UBER would be the platform where other AVs would join through partnerships, similar to the ones that the likes of Waymo and WeRide have made. The partnership with NVIDIA, to me, has more to do with rideshare data, which is vital if the AV revolution has to take off. Given that UBER has one of the biggest global presences relative to its peers, the amount of data that UBER will have, through the NVIDIA partnership, will be a goldmine for any AV player. The ability to collect vast amounts of data and then share it with AV players, which UBER management mentioned during the Q3 earnings call, in my view, is the biggest catalyst that would enable UBER to boost the number of AV partners in the months and years ahead.

The partnership with NVIDIA may be at its early stages and is unlikely to contribute to the company’s bottom-line in the near-term future. However, to me, this partnership is what would enable UBER to remain one step ahead of its rivals when it comes to the battle for AV leadership. It is not surprising, therefore, that CEO Dara Khosrowshahi included AVs as one of his “six areas of strategic focus.”

One of the reasons why UBER’s stock may have declined post its earnings release is the statement made by Mr. Khosrowshahi whereby he said that AVs are unlikely to be profitable for “a few years going forward.” This cannot come as a surprise to investors, and therefore, in my view, it is not a valid reason to sell the stock. It’s very early days for the AV revolution, and as such, the focus of every player as well as every rideshare company would be growth.

I would have understood the market reaction if UBER was an unprofitable company and at the same time was bleeding money. Neither of these is true. At the end of Q3, UBER’s total cash and cash equivalents stood at $9.1 billion, which is about $1.7 billion more than Q2’s total. Moreover, UBER’s trailing 12-month cash flow stands at $8.7 billion. With respect to EPS, even when one excludes the tax revaluation, UBER remains profitable. Finally, adjusted EBITDA margin as a percentage of Gross Bookings came in at 4.5% for Q3, an all-time high for the company. Taking all these together, I believe that the market’s reaction to the statement regarding AVs unprofitability is clearly overdone.

If there is one area that I do find concerning, is that UBER has not further expanded its partnership with Waymo since they last announced a partnership expansion in September of last year. I was hoping that UBER would be Waymo’s partner when it eventually launches in London next year, but as things stand, that has not been the case. Despite partnering with multiple AV companies, UBER’s management disclosed during the earnings call, as I mentioned earlier, that Waymo remains the biggest AV contributor for UBER. Therefore, a lack of further partnership expansion with Waymo is concerning, especially since (LYFT) has also announced a similar partnership in Nashville.

Overall, though, while investors need to monitor the Waymo partnership carefully, all things considered, UBER is certainly heading in the right direction when it comes to positioning itself for the AV revolution. The partnership with NVDA is a game changer in my opinion, and the data that would be collected as part of this partnership would be, in my opinion, the next catalyst for the ridesharing giant.

No Surprises That UBER Looks at Delivery as One of The Areas of Strategic Focus

It is not surprising to me that UBER management has decided to establish the Delivery segment as one of the “six areas of strategic focus.” The segment has exceeded all expectations in the post-COVID era, and Q3 was no different. The segment’s gross bookings in Q3 were the highest in three years. The segment’s Q3 revenues saw their highest growth since Q2 of FY22.

Even as the Mobility segment bounced back, the Delivery segment has sustained its momentum, with the 47% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA providing further evidence of this momentum. The company continues to expand its merchant base, and, the company’s expansion into Grocery & Retail drove the growth in both first-time users, as well as, monthly platform frequency.

In my last article, I talked about how investors shouldn’t underestimate the growth seen in the Delivery segment. And with UBER prioritizing AVs with a renewed focus, the segment should have stronger tailwinds in the coming quarters.

In my last article on UBER, I had expected Q3 gross bookings to come in at $49 billion, which was the midpoint of the company’s guidance at the time. The actual gross bookings of $49.74 billion came in closer to the higher end of the company’s own guidance. Q4 has seasonally been UBER’s strongest quarter, and as such, I have assumed UBER’s Q4 gross bookings to be at the higher end of the company’s guidance, which is $53.75 billion, higher than my previous estimate of $52.9 billion. Total gross bookings for FY25 would then be projected to come in at $193.09 billion, higher than my previous estimate of $191.5 billion.

The company’s Q3 adjusted EBITDA came in at $2.26 billion, which was closer to the high-end of the company’s guidance. The adjusted EBITDA represents 4.55% of the company’s gross bookings. Assuming the adjusted EBITDA represents the same proportion of gross bookings for Q4, Q4 adjusted EBITDA would come in at $2.45 billion. Thus, total adjusted EBITDA for FY25 is now projected to come in at $8.8 billion, the same figure as last time, and represents a y/y growth of 37.5%, which is well within the company’s growth projections of “high 30% to 40%.”

To determine the adjusted EBITDA at the end of FY26, I carried out Monte Carlo Simulations along with a scenario analysis. More specifically, 10,000 simulations were undertaken with the following randomized variables:

  1. Gross bookings growth rate (± 25% standard deviation)
  2. Adjusted EBITDA margin expansion (± 15% standard deviation)
  3. Competitive intensity impact (-10% to +5%)
  4. Drag on margins due to AV investments (-0.5% to -1.5%)
  5. Acceleration in advertising revenues (+20% to +50%)
  6. Macro/Regulatory shocks (-20% to +10%)

For scenario analysis, I assumed Conservative, Base, and Aggressive scenarios with assigned probabilities of 20%, 60%, and 20%, respectively. The impact of variables under each of the scenarios is given below:

The adjusted EBITDA under each of the scenarios based on the results of the Monte Carlo Simulations are as follows:

According to Seeking Alpha, UBER currently trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 21.91x, around the same levels as its long-term median of 21.5x (source: LSEG Data). At this multiple, the company’s enterprise value comes in at $258.32 billion.

I also undertook a separate Monte Carlo Simulation to determine UBER’s ending cash and debt levels under each of the scenarios mentioned earlier.

Based on the Monte Carlo Simulations and the scenario analysis, at the end of 2026, UBER is projected to have a net debt of $1.2 billion. This would imply that UBER’s Equity Value at the end of 2026 would be $256.3 billion.

Finally, I deployed a Monte Carlo Analysis to determine the total number of shares outstanding at the end of 2026, after accounting for stock buybacks, stock-based compensation, and option exercises. The analysis gave the total number of shares outstanding at the end of 2026 as 2.02 billion.

My updated price target for UBER subsequently comes out to be $127, implying nearly a 40% upside from current levels.

Despite the significant upside, the stock has found strong resistance around the $100 level, and its short-term momentum grade stands at B-, according to Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, which is not strong in my view.

Therefore, despite the significant upside, and while I am upgrading my rating from a HOLD to a BUY, I am adding a caveat that a dollar cost-averaging strategy is recommended when it comes to UBER at current levels. Moreover, any significant pullback is certainly a buying opportunity in my view.

Risk Factors

The primary risk to my bull case for UBER is the one that I mentioned before, which is the lack of additional partnerships with Waymo in other cities. As mentioned earlier, UBER management highlighted that the partnership with Waymo has been the biggest contributor to its AV-based trips. As such, a lack of partnership expansion is concerning and is an area that the Bulls will have to monitor going forward.

The other risk is that if the investments in AVs made by UBER turn out to be more aggressive than expected, due to the evolving AV environment, which is likely to pressure future margins. Any loss of momentum in the Delivery business should also be carefully monitored by investors, especially since UBER management announced that the segment would be one of the company’s key areas of strategic focus.

Concluding Thoughts

UBER delivered yet another strong quarter, with a comfortable beat on both the top- and bottom lines. Mobility bounced back, and the Delivery segment maintained its strong momentum. Management also demonstrated a renewed focus on AVs as they announced a hybrid future (Human drivers + AVs) as one of their six areas of strategic focus.

The company’s AV partnerships, especially with Waymo, are already paying off, and its newly announced partnership with NVDA would be a game changer in my view. The data it would collect as part of the partnership is only going to make it a strong attraction for more AV partnerships across the world. Investors, in my opinion, should not focus too much on the profitability of AVs in the near future. It is not prudent to assume that AVs, which are still in their infancy, would be profitable in the blink of an eye. UBER has the resources to invest, and while they scale up their AV ambitions, their core businesses are not only firing on all cylinders but also have the necessary momentum and growth to support this initiative.

The stock is certainly going through some short-term consolidation, especially around the $100 level. Having said that, I do find current levels to be an attractive entry point, given the significant upside from current levels based on my Monte Carlo analysis. However, while I am upgrading my rating on UBER from a HOLD to a BUY, I would be recommending a dollar cost-averaging strategy at current levels, given the subdued momentum and the strong resistance around the $100 level.

UBER’s AV ambitions are heading in the right direction, and the partnership with NVDA should only turbocharge them.

data.title: Exploring The Depth Of Mega Caps: A Look At The S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index
data.description:
By Algreen Bakasa

What lies beneath the headline names in the S&P 100?

Introducing the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index, which measures the performance of companies within the S&P 100, excluding the largest 20 constituents ranked by float-adjusted market capitalization. This blog examines the depth, diversification and shifting leadership that can be obscured by the dominance of the very largest names.

The “Next 80” Make Up a USD 13.4 Trillion Segment

As shown in Exhibit 1, the companies outside the largest 20 make up a USD 13.4 trillion segment, over twice the size of Japan’s equity market and nearly four times larger than the U.K. market and the U.S. mid-cap segment (as measured by the S&P MidCap 400®). Far from a niche slice of the U.S. equity landscape, the “next 80” represent a major portion of the global equity opportunity set.

Beyond its global scale, comparing this segment with the S&P 100 offers additional insight. While the top 20 names often dominate market headlines, the remaining 80 companies represent a meaningful share of the mega-cap U.S. equity segment. Since 2001, these companies have accounted for approximately 45% of the S&P 100’s total weight on average, ranging from 32% to 56% (see Exhibit 2). This variability reflects shifts in mega-cap leadership and reinforces the concept of examining the “next 80” as a distinct lens.

The S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index offers a more evenly distributed profile compared with the S&P 100, which is heavily concentrated in its largest names. Exhibit 3 shows that the top decile of constituents accounted for 55% of the S&P 100’s weight, versus about 22% in the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index. This shows how steeply weight accumulates at the top of the S&P 100, highlighting the extent to which its performance can be driven by a relatively small set of names.

Broadening the Sector Profile

Differences in index size and constituent concentration are also reflected in sector weights. As shown in Exhibit 4, Information Technology represents nearly 25% of the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index, well below the 42% weight in the S&P 100. This lower weight in Tech is paired with greater representation across other sectors, most notably Health Care (18%), Financials (17%) and Industrials (14%). Removing the largest 20 names resulted in a more balanced sector profile, reducing concentration in a few dominant sectors and broadening representation across the drivers of mega-cap U.S. equity performance. While the S&P 100’s Tech weight rose from about 20% in 2010 to over 40% in 2025, the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index increased moderately from roughly 9% to about 20%. Meanwhile, sectors such as Health Care, Financials, Industrials and Materials had higher weights in the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index.

Performance trends aligned with each index’s sector composition. The S&P 100 outperformed in recent years as concentrated mega-cap Tech leadership led performance, while the S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index tended to hold up better during periods when market leadership broadened.

Conclusion

The S&P 100 Ex-Top 20 Select Index offers a complementary lens on the S&P 100 by highlighting a large, diversified segment beyond the top 20 names. Its meaningful scale and broader sector mix provide additional perspective on the composition, concentration dynamics and long-term characteristics of the mega-cap U.S. equity landscape.

For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

data.title: TKO Group Holdings: Rapidly Expanding The Experience Economy Into 2026
data.description:
Barely 3 months after signing its landmark $7.7 billion, 7-year deal with Paramount, TKO Group Holdings (TKO) announced a 5-year media rights deal between Paramount and Professional Bull Riders (PBR)- a subsidiary of TKO acquired in Q1 2025. Evidently, 2026 will be a momentous year for TKO, as it will kick-start the multi-billion-dollar agreements it has made for key assets, such as UFC, WWE, and PBR (as previously stated). Shares of TKO jumped 56% (YoY) and 27.84% (YTD). The company also recently released its Q3 2025 earnings results, and despite the 27% (YoY) revenue decrease in the quarter, I will discuss why the stock is a hold in view of its robust growth into 2026.

There had been fears that the focus on profits would underscore TKO’s global fan base, especially in properties such as WWE, getting into 2025, especially after it launched on ESPN in September 2025. In its Q3 2025 earnings call, TKO (through its CFO) stated,

Live events and hospitality revenue increased 61% to $83 million. The increase was driven by higher ticket sales revenue, reflecting an increase in average ticket price and total attendance, and an increase in site fee revenue.”

To me, this is a case of TKO adjusting its pricing of its tickets in accordance with the market. WWE’s revenues for the 3 months ending on September 30, 2025, outperformed UFC’s revenue by about $77 million and accounted for nearly 36% of WWE’s overall quarterly revenue. In the 9 months to September 30, 2025, WWE’s revenue grew 22.73% (YoY) to $1.35 billion, with the growth including a jump up in live events. While WWE outperformed UFC and IMG across the board (as far as revenues are concerned), UFC still controls key differentiators, such as marketing and partnerships, where it commands a good lead.

In Q3 2025, UFC’s revenue from partnerships outperformed WWE’s by almost 78%, and it grew 20% (YoY) in the 9 months to September 30, 2025. Essentially, global partnership is a significant revenue driver for TKO as a whole, delivering high margins for the business in the form of recurring multi-year revenues.

We have to keep in mind that UFC is discarding pay-per-view revenues after its $7.7 billion deal, which is supposed to begin in 2026. We also need to consider that UFC 306 broke records in 2024, and total revenues in the 9 months to September 2025 outperformed those of 2025 by 7% (YoY), primarily fueled by, among other things, the sponsorship from Saudi Arabia. There have been momentous investments linked with Saudi Arabia, especially in the global sporting scene, and I believe that this partnership with TKO is a precursor to many more. As a reminder, TKO joined the boxing scene (Zuffa Boxing) in H1 2025 following its partnership with Saudi company, General Entertainment Authority, whose launch is expected in January 2026 and is set to be integrated with a prediction feature.

TKO is Entering the Prediction Market Zone

TKO recently announced that it had struck a multi-year deal with global prediction market company, Polymarket, for UFC and Zuffa Boxing. Under the agreement, the two entities will “integrate the prediction market technology directly into the live fan experience.”

The whole point is to grow fan engagement in both UFC and Zuffa boxing through technology. While making this announcement, TKO explained that this agreement will allow live fan predictions to be featured (on both platforms) while the fights are still in progress. Unlike other bookies that integrate sportsbook/betting odds (or probable outcomes), it will feature probabilities implied by the fan base (embedded as a scoreboard). For Polymarket, this deal is an expansion from its traditional politics and global trends to live sporting, especially after landing a partnership agreement with the National Hockey League (NHL). For the NHL, this deal means higher brand exposure and a robust fan engagement platform since the 2025/26 season started in early October 2025. However, TKO is integrating Polymarket into its live events, making it very unique. Then again, when we consider the demographics of TKO’s fan base, which mainly consists of the youth.

According to research, the push by tech giants has forced sports broadcasters to take up mobile-centric approaches that not only appeal to the youth, especially Gen Zs, but are also on demand. There is the aspect of diversity and the dual-screen viewership (watching live events while engaging other fans), features that will be central to Polymarket’s dominance at TKO.

As of 2024, the decentralized prediction market (which houses Polymarket) was valued at $1.4 billion and is expected to reach $95.5 billion by 2035, growing at a CAGR of 46.8%.

Valuation

To evaluate the company, I will use the enterprise value (EV) to sales (EV/S) metric, as it will include cash and the company’s debt in the calculation. TKO’s forward EV/sales ratio stands at 4.95X against the sector median of 1.92X (a difference of 157%). For its FY 2025 revenue, the company gave guidance ranging from $4.69 billion to $4.72 billion (a midpoint of $4.705 billion). To calculate the EV, I will multiply 4.95X by $4.705 billion, giving us $23.3 billion. I will calculate TKO’s market capitalization by subtracting debt ($3.7 billion) from the EV and adding cash ($861.41 million), giving us $20.45 billion. TKO’s total shares outstanding as of October 31, 2025, were 78.922 million. To get the future price, I will divide the market cap by the outstanding share balance, which gives $259.20. As of this writing, TKO’s share price was $183.03, meaning that the stock is undervalued by 41.62% (YoY).

I will compare the performance of the stock with some of its peers.
